meta.name: Moisés Pacheco
meta.role: Owner of Pacheco Power Services ('one call we do it all') — a multi-trade contractor running a crew per discipline (paint, driveways, concrete, foundations, framing, roofing, siding, and more). Takes small jobs solo for cash/Zelle; partners with a licensed American contractor on large permitted (plumbing/electrical/GC) work; supplies hourly labor crews; and subcontracts for other American and Hispanic GCs.
meta.in_target_population: yes — owner of an active multi-trade residential/commercial/industrial contracting business; NON-affiliated, met in passing at Lowe's during a material run. Not a paying customer, and he angled to become a partner/investor — so weigh as discovery, not gate.

interview_integrity.reliability_note: Moisés pressed Eric for the call's intent early ('cuál es la intención, si tienes algún trabajo para mí'), and Eric then disclosed he is building a construction software product; later in the call Eric discussed partnership/investment ($5k of his own money, Delaware incorporation) and named competitors (CompanyCam, AI kitchen renderings). Every positive/future-tense statement after the disclosure is suspect. CRITICAL nuance: Moisés repeatedly angled to become a partner/investor ('¿lo podemos hacer socios?', '¿Es prometedor ese negocio?'), giving him a clear incentive to flatter the idea — yet he still stated the opposite of validation (he doesn't lose money, problems are with people not software, not interested in software). Disconfirmation that survives that incentive is robust. His partnership interest and 'is it promising?' questions are pure interest/noise.

summary: Moisés Pacheco owns Pacheco Power Services, a multi-trade contracting outfit ('one call we do it all') that fields a crew per discipline — paint, driveways, concrete, foundations, framing, roofing, siding and more. He scales his formality to the job: small work is solo, cash/Zelle, minimal paperwork; large permitted work is done in partnership with a licensed American contractor under formal penalty contracts and advance deposits; he also rents out hourly labor crews and subcontracts for other GCs. He presents as financially disciplined (deposits up front, a 15-day rescission-with-forfeiture clause) and asserts he doesn't lose money, and he frames essentially all construction problems as interpersonal — clients and workers — rather than process or software.

commitment_signals.what: Deposit + 15-day rescission clause: won't start work without a deposit; client gets 15 days to back out, forfeiting the deposit
commitment_signals.verbatim: yo a usted no le comienzo un trabajo si usted no, no me da un depósito. Si le doy quince días para que se arrepienta... el depósito obviamente se pierde
commitment_signals.quantified: 15-day rescission window; deposit forfeited on cancellation

commitment_signals.what: Absorbs unbilled estimate/travel time by design and amortizes monthly fixed overhead across multiple concurrent jobs, so no single estimate or job is a standalone loss; gives estimates free on purpose because charging for them would kill lead flow
commitment_signals.verbatim: yo no le voy a cobrar la hora que manejé la estimación que le voy a hacer, me dé o no me dé el contrato, yo no lo voy a cobrar... yo pongo los gastos que yo tengo en el mes. Y si son tres trabajos, yo divido los gastos en esos tres trabajos... al cobrar por las estimaciones no me darían estimaciones
commitment_signals.quantified: overhead split across ~3 jobs/month; ~1hr drive eaten on the estimate discussed

commitment_signals.what: Enforces a paid boundary on chronic-callback ('picky') clients: entertains the warranty callbacks enough to protect the relationship, then charges $250 plus man-hours per visit once the requests exceed reasonable warranty
commitment_signals.verbatim: La próxima vez que usted me cite aquí para algo que vale la pena, el fee va a ser de doscientos cincuenta dólares, y de ahí vamos a partir horas. El tiempo que usted me tenga aquí, porque mi tiempo vale eso
commitment_signals.quantified: $250 base + man-hours per callback beyond reasonable warranty

commitment_signals.what: Real money lost on a one-off uncontracted favor: did a Saturday emergency job for a high-value client with no contract, took a metal rod to the head, went to the ER, and ate his own medical bills — he was paid for the work itself but absorbed the medical cost and had no contract to recover against
commitment_signals.verbatim: un fierro se introdujo en mi cabeza... tuve que irme a emergencias... ¿Cómo yo a esa persona le cobro si no firmamos un contrato?... Sí me hice el trabajo, sí me pagó
commitment_signals.quantified: out-of-pocket ER/medical (amount unstated); work itself was paid

stated_pains.pain: Believes all construction problems are interpersonal — clients and workers — not tooling or process; late materials, bad work, and broken contracts all trace back to a person
stated_pains.verbatim: todos los problemas van a ser con gente, no con el producto, no con el programa, no, con gente. Los problemas van a ser con gente

stated_pains.pain: Demanding/'picky' clients force repeated callbacks (2-5 times) on warranty work after the job is done, eating his time
stated_pains.verbatim: hay clientes muy exigentes que te hacen ir dos, tres, cuatro, cinco veces ya cuando has hecho el trabajo

stated_pains.pain: Uncontracted favor jobs create unbillable liability/risk (injury with no contract to recover against) — but he calls this a special, non-recurring case
stated_pains.verbatim: No es algo que yo hago constantemente... son casos especiales, como un favor

current_workarounds.why_inadequate: He considers it ADEQUATE — it is the reason he says he doesn't lose money. Not a pain point; evidence he has already solved the cancellation/scope-risk problem to his own satisfaction.

current_workarounds.what: Formal signed contract with bilateral penalty clauses + advance deposit for large/serious jobs; partners with a licensed American contractor when permits/licenses are required
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Adequate per him; he only skips it deliberately for special favors (see c4).

current_workarounds.what: $250 + man-hours callback fee to deter chronic-callback clients while preserving the relationship
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Adequate per him — an enforced pricing boundary, not a gap he wants software to fill.

interest_only: Repeatedly asked to become a partner/investor in Eric's product ('¿lo podemos hacer socios?', '¿Es prometedor ese negocio?') — interest in joining the venture, NOT validation of a problem; possibly angling for work or equity. Discount entirely.

Acknowledged AI kitchen-rendering tools and CompanyCam (photo-based estimates) exist, but via other people, not from his own use ('No lo he hecho yo, pero varios me lo han dicho') — awareness, not adoption.
'Por el momento, ahorita no estoy interesado' re: using software on his phone/computer — recorded as a direct statement of NON-demand.

jobs_to_be_done.jtbd: Never get burned by an unreliable client
jobs_to_be_done.desired_outcome: Never start work that won't get paid; never eat the cost of a client backing out

jobs_to_be_done.jtbd: Keep cash flow positive across a variable job portfolio
jobs_to_be_done.desired_outcome: Spread fixed overhead and unbilled estimate time so no single job or estimate is a standalone loss

jobs_to_be_done.jtbd: Cap the cost of high-maintenance clients without losing the relationship
jobs_to_be_done.desired_outcome: Stop unbillable repeat callbacks once they cross a line, while keeping the client

inferred_severity.basis: Stated as worldview, not a felt unmet need — and he explicitly locates the lever in people, not software, so it is not addressable by a product in his view.
Recurring and concrete (2-5 callbacks), but he already enforces a pricing workaround (c3) he is satisfied with — low willingness to pay for a different fix.
Explicitly non-recurring; the loss was out-of-pocket medical on a one-off favor, and he says it would be unjust to pursue recovery — no monetizable, repeatable problem here.

analyst_interpretation.caveats: The dominant finding — no unsolved scope/change-order money pain, and active disinterest in software — is DIRECT from his own words, not inferred. The JTBD framing and all severity ratings are my inference. The idea was pitched mid-call; I weight his disconfirming statements heavily precisely because they cut against his stated incentive to flatter (he wanted to partner/invest). His self-image as 'astuto / never loses money' likely overstates reality — the one concrete probe (the ER accident, c4) shows he does occasionally eat cost — so 'never loses money' is better read as 'disciplined contract hygiene', not literal.

problem_signal.problem_in_their_words: Claims no unsolved problem: 'yo al menos hasta el momento no he perdido dinero porque yo soy un tipo muy astuto'; and 'todos los problemas van a ser con gente, no con el producto, no con el programa' — problems are interpersonal and already managed via deposits, formal contracts, and a callback fee.
problem_signal.relation_to_hypotheses: contradicts A — he meets the explicit falsification condition for Hypothesis A: he has workarounds (deposit + 15-day rescission + formal penalty contracts + $250 callback fee) he is satisfied with, states he does not lose money on scope/contract issues, and is not interested in software.
problem_signal.new_problem_surfaced: Meta-signal (not a product wedge): a non-affiliated contractor frames his pain as relationships/people rather than process or tooling and is uninterested in software — a structural headwind for any software wedge. No new monetizable problem surfaced; worker reliability is the vein he gestures at but never quantifies.

unresolved_questions: Worker/labor reliability is the problem he names but never quantifies — no specific past instance, cost, or frequency was captured.
Whether 'I never lose money' holds under scrutiny or is self-image — c4 (the ER accident) is one counterexample; weather and other accidents were named generically.

Minor transcription ambiguities: 'solo es un ply' = a sheet of plywood (he later says 'llevo una hoja de plywood'); 'Chris' near the plywood line is an unclear referent; the 'vengo a hacer una afirmación' stretch reads as: he drove ~an hour for an estimate, the GC had already hired an electrician, and he stepped in as the available sub.
best_next_question: He says his real problems are with PEOPLE — especially workers — yet never gave a number. Pin him to a specific past instance: 'Cuénteme de la última vez que un trabajador no le llegó o no le cumplió en una obra — esa vez en específico: ¿qué le costó en tiempo y dinero, y qué tuvo que hacer ese día para resolverlo?'

Trabajos grandes, industriales, con-- que ocupen licencia de plomería, de electricidad o una licencia especial de un, eh, general contractor, cosas así, ya entramos los dos ahí. Ah, okey, entiendo. A veces me, a veces me, me contratan para-- por horas por mi personal. Me dicen: "Ocupo cinco personas por horas", yo las meto, pero obviamente, pues yo cobro por ellos y yo les pago a ellos. Y así.
